sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 78 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After RIGHT x8:
- Player at rows 24-27, cols 56-59 (green at cols 56-58, white at col 59)
- White is pointing RIGHT
- Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
- Center blue center at cols 33-34 (inside the frame)
- Left blue center at cols 37-38 (inside the frame)
I moved too far RIGHT! The player is now at cols 56-59, which is far to the right of the frame at cols 32-39. I need to move LEFT to get back to the frame.
But I'm at step 79, which is way over the target of 71. I need to be very efficient now.
Let me move LEFT to get back to the frame's column, then DOWN to get adjacent to the frame.
Current position: rows 24-27, cols 56-59
Frame: rows 36-39, cols 32-39
I need to move LEFT to cols 32-35 (above the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39.
LEFT x24 would get me from cols 56-59 to cols 32-35. That's too many actions.
Let me try LEFT x8 to get to cols 48-51, then LEFT x8 to get to cols 40-43, then LEFT x8 to get to cols 32-35. That's 24 actions, which is too many.
Actually, let me reconsider. The player is at rows 24-27, cols 56-59. The frame is at rows 36-39, cols 32-39. I need to get to the frame and press SPACE.
Maybe I should approach from the right side of the frame. Move DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to get adjacent to the frame from the right.
But the player is at cols 56-59, and the frame is at cols 32-39. I need to move LEFT to get to the frame's column.
Actually, let me try moving DOWN first to get to the frame's row, then LEFT to get adjacent to the frame.
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 56-59
LEFT x17: player at rows 36-39, cols 39-42 (adjacent to frame from right)
SPACE: extend white LEFT toward the frame
But LEFT x17 is too many actions.
Let me try a different approach. Move LEFT to get above the frame, then DOWN to get adjacent to the frame from above.
LEFT x24: player at rows 24-27, cols 32-35 (above the frame)
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 32-35 (inside the frame)
SPACE: extend white DOWN
But LEFT x24 is too many actions.
I'm stuck. Let me try to move LEFT as much as possible and see if I can get to the frame.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the center blue center doesn't need to be collected. Maybe the goal is to collect all blue centers and deliver them to a specific location, and the center blue center is already "delivered" (it's inside the frame).
Looking at the current state:
- Center blue center at cols 33-34 (inside the frame)
- Left blue center at cols 37-38 (inside the frame)
- Top blue center at cols 25-26 (outside the top blue square frame)
So the center and left blue centers are inside the center blue square frame. The top blue center is outside the top blue square frame.
Maybe the goal is to have all blue centers inside their respective frames. The top blue center is outside the top blue square frame, so maybe I need to "return" it to the top blue square frame.
But the top blue center is at cols 25-26, and the top blue square frame is at cols 28-39. The top blue center is to the LEFT of the frame.
Actually, I think the top blue center was collected and moved out of the frame. Maybe the goal is to collect all blue centers and have them inside the white extension or somewhere else.
Let me try a different approach. Let me move LEFT to get back to the center blue square frame and try to collect the center blue center.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions. Let me try to move LEFT efficiently to get back to the frame.
Current position: rows 24-27, cols 56-59
Frame: rows 36-39, cols 32-39
I need to move LEFT to cols 40-43 (right of the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to cols 36-39 (adjacent to frame from right), then SPACE.
LEFT x16: player at rows 24-27, cols 40-43
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 40-43
LEFT x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (inside the frame)
SPACE: extend white LEFT
But this is 24 actions, which is too many.
Let me try a shorter path. Move LEFT to cols 36-39 (above the frame), then DOWN to rows 36-39 (inside the frame), then SPACE.
LEFT x20: player at rows 24-27, cols 36-39
DOWN x4: player at rows 36-39, cols 36-39 (inside the frame)
SPACE: extend white DOWN
But this is 24 actions, which is still too many.
I'm running out of ideas. Let me just try moving LEFT and DOWN to get to the frame and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 24-27, cols 56-59 (white at col 59, pointing RIGHT)
# Frame at rows 36-39, cols 32-39
# Need to get back to the frame to collect the center blue center
# Move LEFT to cols 40-43, then DOWN to rows 36-39, then LEFT to adjacent, then SPACE

# LEFT x16 to get to cols 40-43
result = action(['LEFT']*16)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT x16:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
